claim: The elemental powder milled for 35 h formed multiphase structures consisting of BCC phase (a=0.286 nm, cI2), τ-brass type phase (a=0.872 nm, cI52), and partially ordered B2-type phase (a=0.290 nm, cP2) with trace Si.
evidence_text: The elemental powder was milled for 35 h, leading to the formation of multiphase structures i.e., BCC phase (a = 0.286 nm; cI2), τ-brass type (a = 0.872 nm; cI52) and partially ordered B2-type (a = 0.290 nm; cP2) phases with the trace amount of Si.

claim: The milled powder was thermally stable up to ~500 °C, and formation of Fe5Si3-type phase was evident at ~520 °C.
evidence_text: The milled powder was found to be thermally stable up to ~500 °C, however, the formation of Fe5Si3-type phase was evident at ~520 °C.

claim: The spark plasma sintered sample retained BCC phase (a=0.286 nm), γ-brass type (a=0.872 nm), B2 (a=0.289 nm), and formed Fe5Si3-type silicide phase (a=b=0.667 nm, c=0.468 nm, hP16).
evidence_text: The spark plasma sintered (SPSed) sample was able to retain the BCC phase (a = 0.286 nm), γ-brass type (a = 0.872 nm), B2 (a = 0.289 nm) along with the formation of Fe5Si3 type silicide phase (a = b = 0.667 nm, c = 0.468 nm; hP16).

claim: The SPSed HES sample exhibited low density (~6.49±0.3 g cm−3), high microhardness (~7.8±0.3 GPa), good compressive strength (~2046±160 MPa), and appreciable ductility (~19%).
evidence_text: These SPSed HES sample was found to have low density (~6.49 ± 0.3 g cm−3), high microhardness (~7.8 ± 0.3 GPa) and good compressive strength (~2046 ± 160 MPa) with an appreciable ductility of ~19%.

claim: The melting point predicted by machine learning is 1638 K, while the rule of mixture gives 1620 K, with ML being more precise and accurate.
evidence_text: The melting point obtained from ML and rule of mixture was found to be 1638 K and 1620 K, respectively. ... It was reported that the prediction of the melting point of the HEAs through machine learning approach was more precise and accurate as compared to the rule of mixture [36].

claim: Microhardness test on SPSed sample was performed with Vickers indenter, load 5000 mN, dwell time 20 s, approach speed 80 μm/min, retract speed 16 μm/min, and loading/unloading rate 10,000 mN/min.
evidence_text: The microhardness test was performed with the load of 5000 mN. The dwell time of 20 s, approach speed of 80 μm/min, retract speed of 16 μm/min, loading and unloading rate of 10,000 mN/min, respectively.

claim: Uniaxial compression test on SPSed sample was conducted at 25 °C, sample diameter 4 mm and height 8 mm (ratio 1:2), crosshead speed 0.5 mm/min, strain rate 10^-3 s^-1, repeated three times.
evidence_text: The compression test was done at 25 °C with the sample dimension of diameter (4 mm) to height (8 mm) ratio of 1:2 and at crosshead speed of 0.5 mm/min with the strain rate of 10^-3 s^-1. The test was repeated three times to evaluate the average ultimate compressive strength.

claim: During mechanical alloying, elemental peaks vanish by 5 h; Al diffuses fastest (peak disappears by 15 h); after 25 h, solid solution forms with BCC (a=0.286 nm) and γ-brass type (a=0.872 nm) phases; 35 h milling introduces B2-type ordered phase (a=0.290 nm) with trace Si and no contamination.
evidence_text: Low intensity peaks of all the elements have vanished after 5 h of milling. The intensity of Al diminishes after 15 h of milling indicating that Al has the highest alloying rate. ... The solid solution has been formed after 25 h of milling. The phases formed are BCC (a = 0.286 nm) and γ-brass type (a = 0.872 nm) structure. The milling has been extended up to 35 h, for further nanostructuring ... There is a phase change i.e., B2-type ordered and coexisted with the other phases. ... BCC as a major phase, γ-brass type and B2-type (a = 0.290 nm) phases along with trace amount of Si were formed after 35 h of milling. In the 35 h milled powder, no signature of contamination was observed.

claim: Crystallite size, lattice strain, phase fraction, and dislocation density determined by Rietveld refinement are: at 25 h, BCC 74.4 wt%, γ-brass 21.0%, Si 4.6%, BCC size 17 nm, γ-brass 13 nm, Si 20 nm, BCC strain 10849×10^-4, dislocation 1.94×10^18 m^-2; at 30 h, BCC 73.0%, γ-brass 23.5%, Si 3.5%, BCC size 15 nm, γ-brass 12 nm, Si 13 nm, BCC strain 13195, dislocation 2.67×10^18 m^-2; at 35 h, BCC 64.0%, γ-brass 27.0%, B2 6.0%, Si 3.0%, BCC size 13 nm, γ-brass 10 nm, B2 13 nm, Si 14 nm, BCC strain 17013, γ-brass strain 18829, B2 strain 15052, Si strain 18546, dislocation 3.97×10^18 m^-2.
evidence_text: The crystallite size and lattice strain of the milled samples were calculated using the Rietveld refinement method with the help of GSAS-II (Table 2). The crystallite size was found to vary as a function of milling (Table 2). The crystallite size was found to be tuned in the range of ~13–17 nm and ~10–13 nm for BCC and γ-brass type phase respectively. The crystallite size for Si decreased from 20 nm to 14 nm as milling extended from 25 h to 35 h. The lattice strain increased for all the phases as the milling time progressed.

claim: DTA shows exothermic events at 420 °C (ordering/texturing of γ-brass) and 520 °C (formation of Fe5Si3); annealed XRD reveals stability up to 500 °C, Fe5Si3 appears at 600 °C and grows up to 800 °C, B2 amount increases at 800 °C.
evidence_text: The DTA curve of 35 h milled powder is displayed as Fig. 4, which reveals various exothermic events at 420 °C (693 K) and 520 °C (793 K). ... The exothermic heating event observed at ~420 °C may be corresponding to the ordering and/or texturing of γ-brass type phase ... The heating event at ~520 °C corresponds to the formation of Fe5Si3-type phase ... It is clear that 35 h milled powder is thermally stable up to 500 °C, and afterwards the diffraction peaks of the Fe5Si3 type intermetallic phase (hP16) is evolved at 600 °C and grows further up to 800 °C. At 800 °C, no phase change has occurred but the amount of the B2-type phase is increased.

claim: XRD and Rietveld refinement of SPSed sample show BCC as major phase with partially ordered B2, trace γ-brass, and Fe5Si3 silicide.
evidence_text: XRD pattern of SPSed sample is shown in Fig. 6(a) and its deconvulated image ... The BCC (ferrite) phase was observed as the major phase along with partially ordered B2 and the trace amount of γ-brass type phase as well as Fe5Si3 type phase. Fig. 6(c) shows the Rietveld refinement of the SPSed sample confirming the presence of BCC, B2 type, γ-brass type and Fe5Si3 type silicide.

claim: SEM of SPSed sample (900 °C, 50 MPa) shows four contrasts (white = B2, light grey = BCC, dark grey = γ-brass, black = Fe5Si3); full-area EDS indicates chemically homogeneous distribution of elements.
evidence_text: The SEM micrographs of the SPSed sample at 900 °C and 50 MPa with different magnification are illustrated in Fig. 7(a and b), exhibiting different contrasts i.e., white, black, light and dark grey, which may correspond to the BCC, B2 type, γ-brass type and Fe5Si3 type intermetallic phases, respectively. ... SPSed pellet of non-equiatomic HES, and the distribution of all the elements are uniform and the chemically homogeneous.

claim: EPMA elemental mapping and point WDS confirm white contrast is Al,Ni-rich B2, light grey is Fe,Mn,Ni-rich BCC, and Si-rich regions correspond to Fe5Si3.
evidence_text: The point WDS analysis of these SPSed samples revealed that the white contrast was found to be rich in Al and Ni, which correspond to B2-type phase (spot 1). The light grey contrast were found to be rich in Fe, Mn and Ni, corresponding to BCC (ferritic) phase (spot 2). ... Similarly, the region enriched with Si shows the presence of Fe5Si3-type phase.

claim: The SPSed sample exhibits microhardness 7.8±0.3 GPa, elastic modulus 184±5 GPa, indentation depth ~5.08 μm, compressive strength 2046±160 MPa, and compressive strain ~19%, with no cracking at 5000 mN load.
evidence_text: The load-displacement curve of the SPSed pellet ... The depth of indetation for the SPSed samples was found to be ~5.08 μm. Fig. 9(b) shows the optical image ... The optical micrograph shows no evidence of crack formation even at a load of 5000 mN. ... The evaluated values of the average microhardness and elastic modulus ... are observed approximately 7.8±0.3 GPa and 184±5 GPa, respectively. ... The evaluated values of the average ultimate compressive stress and strain ... are 2046±160 MPa and 19%, respectively.

claim: The SPSed sample exhibits limited plasticity as depicted by the load-displacement curve in Fig. 9(a), with an indentation depth of approximately 5.08 μm.
evidence_text: The depth of indetation for the SPSed samples was found to be ~5.08 μm. Fig. 9(b) shows the optical image of indent on the surface of SPSed pellet.

claim: The partially ordered B2-type structure forms due to highly negative binary mixing enthalpies of Al–Fe and Al–Ni pairs.
evidence_text: In present alloy, the binary enthalpy between the Al–Fe, Al–Ni are highly negative. ... The partially ordered B2-type structure in the alloy was formed due to the binary negative enthalpy.

claim: Carbon dissolves fastest during milling due to its small atomic size and high diffusivity, followed by Al, Mn, and Ni; Si remains undissolved after 35 h because of its lowest self-diffusion coefficient and non-metallic behavior.
evidence_text: But, carbon, being interstitial element dissolved faster as compared to the other alloying elements present which might be due to its less atomic size and high diffusivity. As the milling proceeds, Al dissolved followed by Mn and Ni ... Si remains undissolved after 35 h of milling was due to the lowest self-diffusion coefficient and non-metallic behavior.

claim: After annealing at 400°C, the phase constitution is BCC (57.0%, a=0.286 nm, crystallite size 21 nm), γ-brass type (21.3%, a=0.872 nm, size 12 nm), and B2 (21.7%, a=0.289 nm, size 18 nm).
After annealing at 500°C, the phases are BCC (47.7%, size 23 nm), γ-brass type (36.3%, size 16 nm), and B2 (16.0%, size 20 nm).
At 600°C, Fe5Si3-type silicide appears with 15.5% phase fraction (a=b=0.667 nm, c=0.466 nm, size 15 nm); BCC is 43% (size 26 nm), B2 is 38.5% (size 21 nm), and γ-brass decreases to 3% (size 19 nm).
At 800°C, the phase fractions are BCC 42% (size 28 nm), B2 40% (size 25 nm), Fe5Si3 14% (c=0.468 nm, size 20 nm), and γ-brass 4% (size 22 nm).
The SPSed sample contains BCC 56% (crystallite size 29 nm), B2 34% (27 nm), γ-brass type 1.6% (21 nm), and Fe5Si3 type 8.4% (c=0.468 nm, 23 nm).
Ex-situ XRD (Fig. 5) shows that Fe5Si3-type intermetallic phase forms at 600°C, correlated with the exothermic event at 520°C observed in DTA.
evidence_text: Fig. 5. (a) Ex-situ XRD diffraction pattern ... The formation of Fe5Si3-type intermetallic phase at 600 ◦C is correlated with exothermic event at 520 ◦C (Fig. 4).

claim: The non-equiatomic high entropy steel powder is thermally stable up to approximately 500 °C; at 420 °C the phases are BCC + γ-brass type + B2 with an increased fraction of γ-brass type phase, and at 520 °C Fe5Si3-type silicide forms alongside BCC, γ-brass type and B2 phases.
evidence_text: The alloy is thermally stable up to ~500 °C. At 420 °C, the phases observed to be stable are BCC + γ-brass type + B2-type. But the phase fraction of the γ-brass type structure increased … At 520 °C, the phases are found to be BCC + γ-brass type + B2 and Fe5Si3 type silicide.

claim: X-ray diffraction and Rietveld refinement of the SPSed sample (Fig. 6) reveal a ferritic BCC-type high entropy steel matrix coexisting with B2, γ-brass type structure, and Fe5Si3-type intermetallics.
evidence_text: Fig. 6. (a) XRD pattern of the SPSed sample; (b) Deconvulation of the SPSed sample ranging from 51°-54°; (c) Rietveld refinement plot of the SPSed sample. The ferritic (BCC) type high entropy steel with B2, γ-brass type structure and Fe Si type intermetallics can be depicted.

claim: SEM imaging of the SPSed sample (Fig. 7a,b) shows white (spot 2), light grey (spot 1), and dark grey (spot 3) contrasts; point WDS analysis (Table 5) gives spot 1 composition Fe 32.46, Mn 10.00, Ni 32.09, Al 21.82, Si 2.63 wt% and spot 2 composition Fe 44.53, Mn 21.60, Ni 20.66, Al 7.33, Si 5.88 wt%.
evidence_text: Fig. 7. (a) and (b) SEM image of the SPSed sample of the non-equiatomic high entropy steel at different magnifications; (c) EDS spectrum … Table 5 Elemental composition of SPSed sample … Spot 1 Fe 32.46 Mn 10.00 Ni 32.09 Al 21.82 Si 2.63; Spot 2 Fe 44.53 Mn 21.60 Ni 20.66 Al 7.33 Si 5.88

claim: During nanoindentation of the SPSed sample under a maximum load of 5000 mN, the indentation depth reached approximately 6 µm (Fig. 9a).
evidence_text: Depth of Indentation (µm) … 0 0; 2 ~500; 4 ~1500; 6 ~5000

claim: The SPSed high entropy steel exhibits an ultimate compressive strength of approximately 2046 ± 160 MPa at a strain of about 0.20 (Fig. 9c).

Theoretical thermodynamic criteria and calculated parameters for the present FeMnNiAlSiC high entropy steel are ΔHmix = -8.4 kJ/mol, ΔSconf = 12.4 J/K·mol, atomic size difference δ = 7.6 %, Ω = 2.4, and valence electron concentration VEC = 6.9 (Table 6).
evidence_text: Thermodynamic parameter … Present work -8.4 12.4 7.6 2.4 6.9

claim: CALPHAD modeling (Fig. 10, Table 8) predicts that at 500 °C the equilibrium phases are BCC_A2#1 (0.69 mole fraction), B2 (0.28), BCC_A2#2 (0.02), and M3Si (0.01), while at 1100 °C the phases are liquid (0.40), BCC_A2#1 (0.48), and B2 (0.12).
evidence_text: Table 8 Single-point equilibrium … 1100 °C Liquid 0.40; BCC_A2#1 0.48; B2 0.12 … 500 °C BCC_A2#1 0.69; B2 0.28; BCC_A2#2 0.02; M3Si 0.01

claim: The differences between experimental phases formed during annealing/SPS and CALPHAD-predicted phases are attributed to the use of the TCFE8 database instead of the TCHEA3 database.
evidence_text: The phases formed during the annealing and SPSed were also correlated with the CALPHAD (Thermo-Calc) modelling predicted phases. Some difference was there … This may be due to the fact that the CALPHAD database TCFE8 was used instead of the TCHEA3.

claim: The total yield stress of the SPSed sample is expressed as σy = σo + Δσgb + Δσp + Δσd + Δσss (Equation 5), where the terms represent frictional, grain boundary, precipitate, dislocation, and solid solution strengthening.
evidence_text: The total yield stress can be expressed as: $$\sigma_ {\mathrm{y}} = \sigma_ {\mathrm{o}} + \Delta \sigma_ {\mathrm{gb}} + \Delta \sigma_ {\mathrm{p}} + \Delta \sigma_ {\mathrm{d}} + \Delta \sigma_ {\mathrm{ss}}$$ (5) where, σ0 is frictional stress, Δσgb is grain boundary strengthening, Δσp is precipitate strengthening, Δσd is dislocation strengthening and Δσss is solid solution strengthening.

claim: Dislocation strengthening using Bailey-Hirsch relation yields 687 MPa for BCC (dislocation density 4.46×10^15 m−2) and 590 MPa for B2 (3.36×10^15 m−2).
evidence_text: The contribution of the dislocation strengthening was calculated using the Bailey-Hirsch relationship ... ρdd ... 4.46×10^15 m−2 and 3.36×10^15 m−2, for BCC and B2-type, respectively. The strength value was found to be 687 MPa and 590 MPa for BCC and B2-type respectively.

claim: Figure 9(e,f) and Supplementary Table S1 show that the present quinary low-density HES with BCC+B2+silicide exhibits excellent compressive strength and microhardness compared to reported HEAs and conventional steels.
evidence_text: A comprehensive comparison of the present alloy with the high entropy alloys is shown in Fig. 9(e and f). The quinary low-density high entropy steel with the formation of BCC and B2 with silicide phase showed the excellent mechanical properties i.e., compressive strength and microhardness as compared to the HEAs and conventionally steel.

claim: The SPSed high entropy steel exhibits limited ductility of ~19%.
evidence_text: these SPSed HES were found to have a limited ductility of ~19%.

claim: The in-situ formation of silicide in the present alloy plays a crucial role in strengthening, enhancing microhardness and compressive strength while retaining good ductility.
evidence_text: In the present alloy, the in-situ formation of the silicide has played a crucial role to strengthen the alloy i.e., microhardness and compressive strength with good amount of ductility.
